critical_issues: Verification is shallow: 'poll list_screens up to 3 polls' confirms a screen EXISTS but not whether it reflects the ONE IDEA or broke anti-generic patterns — the agent can check a box without doing good design
Memory write instructions are vague ('what worked vs failed') with no structured format, so future runs get inconsistent context and can't build on prior learnings systematically

No explicit fallback for the async poll failure case — if 3 polls return nothing, the harness says 'NEVER retry same screen' but doesn't say what to do next (skip? report? try a different prompt?), leaving the agent stuck

Phase turn estimates (3-5, 10-20, 5-10, 5-10) sum to 23-45 turns but 100 turns are budgeted — the gap between budget and protocol creates no pressure to be efficient, and agents tend to over-generate when turns are unconstrained

improvements.current: Poll list_screens after EVERY generate. Up to 3 polls. No exceptions. Build a verification table before writing the report. A run with no verified screens is a failed run.
improvements.suggested: Poll list_screens after EVERY generate. Up to 3 polls. If 3 polls return nothing, skip that screen, log it as FAILED in the verification table, and continue. Verification is not just existence — after getting the screen, score it: (a) Does it reflect the ONE IDEA? (b) Did it break 3+ anti-generic patterns? (c) Is the layout asymmetric or does it default to centered? If score < 2/3, trigger an edit_screens pass before moving on.
improvements.reason: Current verification only checks existence. The agent can log a screen as verified while it's a generic hero+features layout. Quality gates need to be explicit and tied to the design criteria already defined in the harness.

improvements.current: Update at end with: 1. Project ID and screen IDs 2. The ONE IDEA that worked (or didn't) 3. Which anti-generic patterns were successfully broken 4. Visual references that produced good results 5. Stitch prompt patterns that worked vs failed
improvements.suggested:
Update at end using this exact structure:
```
## Run: [date]
- projectId: [id]
- screens: [id1 (HOOK), id2 (PROOF), ...]
- ONE IDEA: [exact phrase used] — worked: yes/no — why: [one sentence]
- Anti-generic wins: [list exactly which checklist items were broken]
- References used: [site] for [what] — result: [good/bad]
- Prompt pattern: [what the opening sentence was] — output quality: [1-5]
- Avoid next time: [specific thing that produced generic output]
```
improvements.reason: Structured memory format makes future runs actionable. Free-form memory notes accumulate without being scannable, so the agent re-learns lessons instead of building on them.

improvements.current: ASYNC — empty response is normal. Poll list_screens after every generate. Up to 3 polls. NEVER retry same screen.
improvements.suggested: ASYNC — empty response is normal. Poll list_screens after every generate. Up to 3 polls with 5-second waits between. If screen not found after 3 polls: log as FAILED, note the prompt that failed in memory, and proceed with remaining screens. Do NOT retry the same prompt — generate a different screen instead. A failed screen does not block the run.
improvements.reason: Current instruction creates a dead end: the agent knows not to retry but doesn't know what to do next. Without an explicit continue path, agents often either retry anyway or stall.

improvements.current: Phase 1: Creative Direction (3-5 turns) ... Phase 2: Generate + Verify (10-20 turns) ... Phase 3: Explore Alternatives (5-10 turns) ... Phase 4: Verification + Report (5-10 turns)
improvements.suggested: Add at the top of the section: **Turn budget: 100 total. Target: Phase 1 ≤10, Phase 2 ≤40, Phase 3 ≤25, Phase 4 ≤15. If you reach 70 turns without entering Phase 4, skip Phase 3 entirely and go directly to verification.** This prevents the most common failure: running out of turns before the report.
improvements.reason: Without explicit budget allocation per phase, agents burn turns in Phase 2-3 (generation is fun, iterating is easy) and then hit the wall before completing the report. The guardrail forces phase discipline.

improvements.current: If you can't fill this in with something SPECIFIC and SURPRISING, stop and think harder.
improvements.suggested: If you can't fill this in with something SPECIFIC and SURPRISING after two attempts, write down the 3 most generic versions you considered, then explicitly invert one of them. Example: generic = 'dark background with glowing code', inverted = 'white background where the only dark element is the code block — everything else is washed out'. Submit the inverted version.
improvements.reason: Telling the agent to 'think harder' produces no change in behavior. Giving it a concrete escape hatch (inversion technique) when stuck creates a deterministic path out of the generic trap.

verdict: The harness has strong domain encoding and clear creative philosophy but needs quality-gated verification (not just existence checks), structured memory format, and explicit turn budget allocation per phase before it will consistently produce high-scoring runs.
